perturbation.original_text: “IPR” means all copyright, patent rights, design rights, trade marks, layout -design rights, trade secrets, proprietary rights and any other intellectual and industrial property rights whether registered or unregi stered, in all countries of the world , and shall include without limitation, any pending patent applications and/or any part thereof .
perturbation.changed_text: “IPR” means registered patents and trademarks held by ISCA.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of IPR covered a wide range of intellectual property rights. The modified version drastically narrows it to only registered patents and trademarks. This creates a contradiction with Section 7(1), which states that the Vendor does not acquire any right, title, or interest in 'ISCA IPR.' The change limits the scope of protection, potentially allowing the Vendor to claim rights over other forms of intellectual property not explicitly covered in the revised IPR definition, leading to a direct in-text contradiction and uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Acknowledgements Vendor acknowledges and agrees that: (1) it does not acquire by implication or otherwise any right, title or interest i n or to the Confidential Information and ISCA IPR, including any licence; (2) nothing in this Agreement nor in any discussions nor disclosures made pursuant to this Agreement shall be deemed a commitment to disclose any information to the Vendor or to engage i n any business relationship, contract or future dealing with the Vendor ; (3) nothing herein shall create or be deemed to create a partnership or joint venture or relationship of principal and agent or any manner of business relationship whatsoever between th e Parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'IPR' broadly, including various intellectual property rights. The changed text significantly narrows the definition to only registered patents and trademarks held by ISCA. Section 7(1) states that the Vendor does not acquire any right to 'ISCA IPR'. By narrowing the definition of IPR, the changed text creates a contradiction because the vendor may now be able to claim rights over intellectual property that falls outside the limited definition of 'IPR' but was previously covered, thus contradicting the intent of Section 7(1).

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 In the event that the Parties do not execute any a greement for the Services or upon demand by ISCA , the Vendor undertakes to forthwith: (1) return all Confidential Information (including all originals and copies) to ISCA ; or (2) where (1) is not possible, at ISCA’s option , destroy, erase, delete, or securely dispose of such Confidential Information, and to furnish ISCA with a written confirmation , certifying that no copies of the Confidential Information have been made, reproduced, retained , or distributed by itself, its employees , or any third parties , without prejudice to the obligations of confidentiality hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 In the event that the Parties do not execute any agreement for the Services or upon demand by ISCA, the Vendor will return readily available documents marked confidential to ISCA. The specifics of document return will be determined by ISCA.
perturbation.explanation: This revision replaces the mandatory return or destruction of all Confidential Information with a vague obligation to return 'readily available documents marked confidential'. It gives ISCA unilateral control over 'the specifics of document return.' This change contradicts the original intent of comprehensively protecting ISCA's confidential information as defined in Clause 1, potentially leaving sensitive data unprotected, contradicting the overall purpose of the NDA. The change creates ambiguity in the Vendor's obligations, impacting enforcement, especially since Confidential Information definition was made less comprehensive.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 The Vendor hereby agrees to maintain as confiden tial and agrees not to use except for the Limited Purpose, any part or the whole of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the Vendor's obligation to protect ISCA's confidential information. The original text mandates the return or destruction of *all* Confidential Information. The changed text limits this to 'readily available documents marked confidential' and gives ISCA control over specifics. This contradicts Clause 2.1, which requires the Vendor to maintain as confidential *any part or the whole* of *any* Confidential Information. The modified text creates a loophole where the Vendor could potentially retain and use confidential information that isn't 'readily available' or properly marked, directly conflicting with the intent and wording of Clause 2.1.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 The protection to be accorded to t he Confidential Information to be disclosed hereunder does not and shall not extend to any information which: (1) at the Effective Date is public knowledge or which subsequently becomes public knowledge other than by any breach of the terms of this Agreement; (2) is proven by Vendor by written evidence to have been already known to the Vendor at the Effective Date and not acquired directly or indirectly from ISCA , and such information has been obtained without restrictions on disclosure to others; or (3) is required to be disclosed by applicable law or order of a court of competent jurisdiction or recognised stock exchange or government department or agency with valid authority, provided that prior to such disclosure the Vendor consults with ISCA as to the proposed form , scope, nature, and purpose of the disclosure, co -operates with ISCA to limit the extent of the disclosure, and permits ISCA time to obtain legal remedies to maintain such Confidential Information in confidence.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 The protection to be accorded to the Confidential Information to be disclosed hereunder does not and shall not extend to any information which is public knowledge.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clauses that allow the Vendor to prove prior knowledge or the requirement to disclose due to legal obligations, the modified clause now states that only information that is public knowledge is excluded from protection. The omission creates a contradiction if the Vendor is legally required to disclose confidential information, as they are now unable to, according to the terms of the contract. The original clause provided an exception for legally mandated disclosures, but with its removal, the Vendor would be in breach of contract if it complied with the law.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. Governing Law 10.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accord ance with Singapore law. 10.2 The Parties hereby submit to the jurisdiction of the Singapore courts.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits exceptions for legally required disclosures and prior vendor knowledge, which were present in the original text. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text (Governing Law), because if Singapore law requires the vendor to disclose confidential information, the changed text would prevent them from doing so without breaching the contract, as it now excludes all but 'public knowledge'. This is a clear, direct, and impactful contradiction, because the original contract allowed the vendor to abide by law and comply with the contract but the modified contract forces the vendor to break the contract to abide by law.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Vendor shall not collect, use , process and/or disclose any Personal Data of ISCA’s clients or customers (collectively “ Customers ”) or of ISCA’s employees, without the prior written consent of ISCA. 6.2 Vendor shall at all times comply with the provisions of the PDPA and not cause ISCA t o be in breach of its obligations under the PDPA, including in relation to Personal Data of Customers and ISCA’s employees.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Vendor shall obtain ISCA’s consent before collecting, using or disclosing any personal data of ISCA’s employees or customers.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit requirement for the Vendor to comply with the PDPA, this creates a contradiction. While clause 6.1 states they need consent before collecting/using personal data, the explicit mention of the PDPA is removed. The omission creates a contradiction with the overall intention of protecting personal data as implicitly stated in 6.1 and other sections referencing the PDPA, causing potential non-compliance with Singapore law.
perturbation.contradicted_text: “PDPA ” means the Singapore Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (No. 26 of 2012) and all subsidiary legislation and guidelines issued pursuant thereto . “Personal Data ” shall have the same meaning as that set out in the PDPA.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly requires compliance with the PDPA, which is defined in the 'Contradicted Text'. The 'Changed Text' removes this explicit requirement, only stating that ISCA's consent is needed. This creates an in-text contradiction because while consent is required, the removal of the explicit PDPA compliance contradicts the implicit requirement in the contradicted text that defines and references the PDPA, potentially leading to non-compliance with the PDPA regulations. This aligns with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Employee Undertakings 3.1 The Vendor shall, before any permitted disclosures are made to any employees pursuant to Clause 2.2(1) , inform such employees of the Vendor ’s obligations under this Agreement and bind such employees to observe the terms of this Agreement. 3.2 For the avoidance of doubt, the Vendor shall be responsible for ensuring its employees’ strict compliance with the terms of this Agreement. Any non -compliance with the terms of this Agreement by an employee of the Vendor shall be deemed a breach of this Agreement by Vendor .
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Employee Undertakings 3.1 The Vendor shall inform their employees of the confidential nature of the ISCA information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly requires the vendor to bind its employees to the terms of the NDA before disclosing confidential information. The modified text only requires informing the employees of the 'confidential nature' of the information, removing the binding obligation. This omission weakens the confidentiality protections and creates a potential contradiction because the Vendor is still held responsible for breaches by employees (as implied in the original 3.2 but now undermined by 3.1's reduced obligation), making enforcement uncertain. Clause 3.2 is effectively contradicted by this weakened obligation, since there is no explicit process to bind employees to the original contract's clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Employee Undertakings 3.1 The Vendor shall inform their employees of the confidential nature of the ISCA information.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the vendor to 'bind such employees to observe the terms of this Agreement'. The changed text only requires informing employees of the 'confidential nature of the ISCA information'. This omission creates a contradiction because the Vendor is still held responsible for breaches by employees, creating uncertainty since there is no explicit process to bind employees to the original contract's clause, making enforcement uncertain. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists. The Changed Text and the Contradicted text are in the same location, however the change creates the contradiction that the question is asking for.
